speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. If you already know the everyday Git moves like clone, branch, commit, push, and pull, this lesson is about the next layer up: the commands people reach for when real work gets messy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the part where Git starts feeling powerful and a little scary.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. We're covering the operations that come up constantly in open source and team workflows: grabbing one commit without merging a whole branch, cleaning up history before a pull request, undoing mistakes safely, understanding detached HEAD, force pushing without causing trouble, finding the exact commit that introduced a bug, cleaning untracked files, and figuring out why a protected branch refuses your push.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the appendix this comes from is organized around three ways to do things, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Most sections show a VS Code path, a GitHub CLI path with gh when that makes sense, and the Git CLI path. Different tools, same underlying Git concepts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's also keep the accessibility guidance in view, because this material is meant to work as a reference too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If you're reading along with the markdown, each operation is its own numbered section. Screen reader users can jump by heading between operations, and then jump among the tool-specific subheadings. The table of contents links go directly to each operation. Low vision users get a very consistent layout: what the command is, when to use it, then the tool-specific steps. And there's a quick reference table near the bottom for fast lookup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the structure itself is part of the teaching.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with cherry-pick. Cherry-pick means: take one specific commit from somewhere else and apply that commit onto the branch you're on now.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not merge the branch. Not rebase the branch. Just copy one change.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the idea. Think of it as surgically lifting one commit out of another branch. You'd use it when a bug fix exists on a feature branch and main needs that fix immediately, or when a teammate has one useful commit but you don't want all their in-progress work, or when you committed to the wrong branch and need that commit somewhere else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before you can cherry-pick, you need the commit identifier, the SHA.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. In VS Code, one path is the Timeline view. You can open the Timeline, move to the branch that contains the commit, inspect the history there, and copy the SHA from the details.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from the terminal?

speaker: ALEX
text: A very direct command is to view another branch's history without switching to it: git log, the branch name, and oneline output. That gives you short commit IDs followed by commit messages. So you might see something like a short SHA, then "Fix null pointer error in auth module," then another SHA for "Add unit tests for login." The short SHA is typically the first seven characters, and that's often enough for local operations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "without switching to it" part is nice. People often think they have to check out the other branch just to inspect it.

speaker: ALEX
text: You usually don't.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay. Once I have the SHA, how do I actually do the cherry-pick?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, open the Command Palette, type cherry, choose the Git cherry-pick command, paste or type the SHA, and VS Code applies it to your current branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And gh?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI does not itself perform cherry-pick, because cherry-pick is a local Git operation. But gh can help you discover the commit SHA through the GitHub API. Then you still use regular git cherry-pick locally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So gh helps find; git helps apply.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. At the Git CLI, you can cherry-pick a single commit. You can also cherry-pick a range of commits using the older-dot-dot-newer syntax, where the first endpoint is exclusive. And you can use no-commit if you want Git to apply the changes without creating the commit immediately, which gives you a chance to review or modify the result first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That no-commit option sounds useful if I'm not totally sure the commit belongs exactly as-is.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It gives you a checkpoint before the final commit is created.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about conflicts?

speaker: ALEX
text: Cherry-pick can conflict for the same reason merges do: the target branch and that commit both touched overlapping code. Git pauses, marks the conflicting files, and you resolve them. In VS Code you can use the conflict editor. After resolving, stage the fixed files. Then continue the cherry-pick. If you decide this was a mistake, abort and Git returns you to where you started.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, the appendix had a practical tip here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: if a cherry-pick stops because of a conflict, run git status. It tells you which files need attention. The conflict markers in the files are plain text, so they read line by line in a straightforward way.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a Copilot angle.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If you're unsure whether a commit is the right one to cherry-pick, you can inspect it with git show and paste that output into Copilot Chat with a question like, "What does this commit do, and should I cherry-pick it onto my branch?" And if a conflict appears, Copilot can help explain the conflict or suggest a resolution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's move to interactive rebase, one of the most useful and most misunderstood Git tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the one people use to make their branch history look less chaotic before opening a pull request.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Interactive rebase lets you rewrite your local commit history before it becomes shared history. You can reorder commits, squash several tiny commits together, rewrite commit messages, drop a commit entirely, or pause to edit one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the golden rule first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Only rewrite commits that have not yet been pushed to a shared branch, unless you fully understand the consequences and are prepared to force push safely afterward. Rewriting public history can create problems for collaborators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So interactive rebase is ideal for cleaning up your own branch before the branch becomes everybody else's problem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you start one?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, you'll generally open the integrated terminal because full interactive rebase is really a terminal workflow. A common command is rebase interactively on HEAD tilde 3, which means, "open the last three commits for editing." Another common form is interactive rebase against main, meaning, "rewrite everything on my branch since I diverged from main."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when that opens, what exactly am I looking at?

speaker: ALEX
text: A list of commits, one per line. Each line begins with a command word such as pick. Pick means keep the commit as-is. Squash means combine this commit into the one above it. Fixup also combines it into the one above but discards this commit's message. Reword keeps the commit but asks you to edit the message. Drop removes the commit entirely. Edit pauses at that commit so you can amend it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the rebase file is really a little instruction sheet to Git.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's a common cleanup example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Suppose you made one real commit and then two typo-fix commits. You can leave the first as pick and change the next two to squash. Save and close the file. Git then opens another editor asking for the final combined commit message. You write one clean message, save, and now those three commits become one tidy commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And reword is for when the code is fine but the message is vague.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Change pick to reword for that commit, save, and Git prompts you to edit the message.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also included an important editor tip.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If rebase opens in vim and that's not comfortable, especially for accessibility, set your Git editor to VS Code with core.editor set to code wait. Then future interactive rebases open in VS Code, which is often much more screen-reader-friendly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if I get nervous halfway through?

speaker: ALEX
text: Abort the rebase, and Git returns to the pre-rebase state. If you hit a conflict mid-rebase, resolve the conflict, stage the files, and continue the rebase. The pattern is similar to cherry-pick and merge: fix, stage, continue. If needed, abort.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next is git reset, which is all about undoing, but at different depths.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the one where the modes matter a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much. Reset can move HEAD and optionally change the staging area and working tree. The three classic modes are soft, mixed, and hard.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's translate that into plain language.

speaker: ALEX
text: Soft reset moves the branch pointer back but keeps your changes staged. Mixed reset, which is the default, moves the branch pointer back and unstages the changes, but keeps them in your files. Hard reset moves the branch pointer back and discards the changes from both staging and working tree.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So soft is the gentlest undo, mixed is a deeper undo but keeps the work, and hard is destructive.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Hard reset is the dangerous one. The appendix explicitly warns that discarding changes with hard reset may be impossible to recover without reflog, and even then you're depending on local history that may not last forever.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How might someone do this in VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix includes VS Code routes for undoing the last commit through the Source Control panel and through the Command Palette. Those are friendly entry points when you want to undo a recent commit without memorizing flags.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And at the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are several common patterns. Undo the last commit but keep changes staged: soft reset to HEAD tilde 1. Undo the last commit but keep changes unstaged: mixed reset to HEAD tilde 1, or simply reset HEAD tilde 1 since mixed is the default. Undo the last three commits but keep all changes unstaged: reset mixed to HEAD tilde 3. Undo the last commit and discard the changes entirely: hard reset to HEAD tilde 1. You can also reset to a specific commit SHA if you want to move directly to some earlier point.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's a separate but related task here too: unstaging.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Sometimes you do not want to undo a commit at all. You simply staged the wrong file. In VS Code, you can unstage from the Source Control interface. In Git CLI, you can unstage a specific file by resetting that file, or unstage everything by resetting with no path. That removes content from the staging area but leaves the working tree unchanged.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That distinction is huge: unstaging is not deleting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It just says, "don't include this in the next commit yet."

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's compare reset with revert, because people mix them up constantly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Revert is the safe undo for shared branches.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Reset rewrites history. Revert preserves history by adding a new commit that undoes an earlier commit. That makes revert the safer choice when the branch is already shared, published, or protected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I accidentally broke main and the bad commit is already on the remote, I should usually revert, not reset.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. In VS Code, there are interface-based ways to revert a commit. In the Git CLI, the standard form is revert followed by the commit SHA. By default that opens an editor so you can confirm or edit the revert commit message. If you want the default message without opening the editor, there's a no-edit form. And if you want Git to apply the revert changes without committing yet, there's a no-commit form so you can review or change the message yourself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also mentions reverting a merge commit, which is a special case.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Reverting a merge commit requires specifying which parent should be treated as the mainline. That's because a merge has more than one parent, so Git needs to know which side you want to preserve when constructing the inverse change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when do I use revert versus reset in one sentence?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use reset for local history cleanup before sharing; use revert when history is already shared and you need a safe, auditable undo.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's talk about tags.

speaker: JAMIE
text: These are the labels you attach to important moments in history, like releases.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A tag marks a specific commit with a memorable name. There are two main kinds here: lightweight tags and annotated tags.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the difference?

speaker: ALEX
text: A lightweight tag is basically just a named pointer to a commit. An annotated tag stores extra metadata like tagger, date, and a message, which is why annotated tags are generally recommended for releases.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create them?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, the Command Palette can help create tags. With GitHub CLI, one convenient path is to create a release through GitHub's API or release tooling, which can create both the underlying tag and a GitHub Release in one step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's an important distinction: a Git tag and a GitHub Release are related but not identical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The release is a GitHub platform object; the tag is a Git object. Some GitHub workflows create both together.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the plain Git CLI options?

speaker: ALEX
text: Create a lightweight tag at the current commit with just the tag name. Create an annotated tag with the a flag and a message. Tag a specific older commit by giving the tag name and the commit SHA. You can list all tags, list tags matching a pattern, and show the details of an annotated tag.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Creating the tag locally doesn't automatically publish it, though.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You then push either one specific tag or all local tags to GitHub. And if you need to delete a tag, there are two separate steps to think about: delete the local tag, and if it was already published, delete the remote tag on the GitHub remote as well.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now for detached HEAD.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This phrase sounds mysterious the first time you hear it.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does, but the core idea is simple: normally HEAD points to a branch name, and that branch name points to a commit. In detached HEAD, HEAD points directly to a commit instead of a branch. You can inspect history there, but if you make new commits and then move away without saving them onto a branch, they become easy to lose track of.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do people end up there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually by checking out a specific commit SHA or sometimes a tag, instead of checking out a branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I just want to get back to normal, what do I do?

speaker: ALEX
text: If you didn't make any commits you care about, simply switch back to a real branch, often the branch you were on before.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if I did make commits while detached?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then create a new branch at the current detached position. That anchors those commits to a branch, making them safe. After that, you're on a real branch again and the work is no longer floating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And VS Code can help show this state too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. VS Code's branch and source control indicators can reveal that you're detached, and you can create or switch branches from the UI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's tackle force pushing safely, because this is where good intentions can become team problems.

speaker: JAMIE
text: We need the difference between force and force-with-lease.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Force push overwrites the remote branch with your local branch. Force-with-lease is the safer variant: it will refuse to overwrite the remote if someone else has pushed changes since your last fetch. So it acts like a seatbelt.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When would I need any force push at all?

speaker: ALEX
text: Most commonly after rewriting commit history on your own branch, especially after interactive rebase. Your local branch now has a different history than the remote branch, so a normal push is rejected. Force-with-lease is then the typical way to update the remote copy of your branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But not usually on shared branches like main.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. This is usually for your own feature branch, not protected shared branches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do the tools fit here?

speaker: ALEX
text: VS Code offers push flows, but for force pushing, the appendix explicitly says to use git directly. GitHub CLI likewise is not the place for the actual force-push flag; use Git CLI.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the key commands?

speaker: ALEX
text: Safe force push is push with force-with-lease. There's also a way to inspect what will happen before pushing, and there is unconditional force, plain force, which the appendix says to avoid unless you know exactly what you're doing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you walk through the common rebase-plus-force-push sequence?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. One: you're on your feature branch and rebase it onto main to pick up the latest changes. Two: resolve any conflicts and continue the rebase. Three: because the remote still has the old pre-rebase history, you push your branch with force-with-lease so the remote branch is updated safely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the safe pattern is: rewrite locally, verify, then force-with-lease, not plain force.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's look at git bisect, which is one of Git's most brilliant debugging tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the "find the exact bad commit" feature by binary search.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Instead of checking every commit one by one, bisect repeatedly chooses a midpoint between a known bad commit and a known good commit. You test that midpoint and tell Git whether it's good or bad. Each answer cuts the search space roughly in half.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you start?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, start bisect mode. Then mark the current broken state as bad. Then tell Git a commit you know was good, maybe by SHA, tag, or branch name from before the bug appeared.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After that, Git checks out a midpoint and asks me to judge it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If the bug is present at that midpoint, mark it bad. If the bug is not present, mark it good. Git moves again. You keep repeating until Git identifies the first bad commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when I'm done?

speaker: ALEX
text: End the bisect session to return to your original branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also mentions automation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you have a reliable test script that exits successfully when things are good and fails when things are bad, you can ask Git to run that script automatically at each step. That turns bisect into a mostly hands-off search.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there was a note later about Copilot helping if the bisect itself becomes confusing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Copilot can help interpret odd outputs or help debug why your test script isn't behaving consistently during bisect.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next up is git clean, which sounds mild but can be destructive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is for deleting untracked files, not tracked changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Clean removes files Git is not tracking. That often means build artifacts, generated files, or scratch files cluttering your working directory. The big rule here is: always dry-run first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because otherwise you might delete something you forgot wasn't tracked.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The dry-run form shows what would be deleted without actually deleting it. The appendix even notes an equivalent dry-run spelling, so learners recognize both forms.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the common variations?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can remove untracked files only, remove untracked files and directories, remove untracked plus ignored files--which is especially dangerous because that includes things listed in gitignore, like build outputs--and use interactive mode so Git prompts you before deleting items.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the option meanings matter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix explicitly explains the common flags: n for dry run, f for force, because Git usually requires force before deleting, d for directories, and x for ignored files too.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there a VS Code angle?

speaker: ALEX
text: There is mention of VS Code, but git clean is fundamentally more of a command-line task. The key teaching point is understanding what counts as untracked and using dry-run before any destructive clean.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's talk about branch protection, because sometimes Git isn't rejecting you due to a mistake in your command. Sometimes the repository rules are doing their job.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the "why can't I push to main?" lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Branch protection can block direct pushes, block merges until reviews pass, require status checks or CI, require up-to-date branches, and enforce other repository policies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a push or merge is blocked, it may be policy, not a broken workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And the correct flow for protected branches is very consistent. Never work directly on main. Create a branch. Make your changes and commit them. Push your branch, not main. Open a pull request on GitHub. Then wait for reviews and automated checks to pass, and merge through the pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That line from the appendix is subtle but important: protection rules often apply to the merge into the protected branch, not to your ability to push your own feature branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if my branch is out of date with main and GitHub says it must be updated?

speaker: ALEX
text: You usually have two options. Option A is merge main into your branch. That creates a merge commit and is often straightforward. Option B is rebase your branch onto main. That gives a cleaner history, but because it rewrites your branch history, it usually requires a force-with-lease push afterward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And those can be done in VS Code, plain Git CLI, or in some cases via GitHub tooling.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix includes VS Code and CLI approaches, and also notes GitHub CLI routes for updating a pull request branch from the GitHub side when that workflow is available.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do I check what branch protection rules are actually active?

speaker: ALEX
text: Through GitHub CLI, you can query branch protection information for a branch like main. And on GitHub.com, repository settings expose the rules in the branch protection or ruleset interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the real skill isn't just knowing Git commands. It's recognizing when repository governance is part of the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely.

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix also includes a quick reference table of advanced commands.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is not new content so much as a compact lookup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It gives one-row-per-operation summaries so you can quickly remind yourself which command family fits which problem: cherry-pick for transplanting a commit, interactive rebase for cleaning history, reset for local undo, revert for shared-history undo, tags for marking commits, bisect for bug hunting, clean for untracked files, and so on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we finish, there's a modern companion topic: using GitHub Copilot for Git operations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that this section is not "let Copilot do magic," but "use Copilot to understand and choose better."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Copilot can be especially helpful in a few focused situations. One is resolving merge conflicts. You can show it the conflict markers and ask it to explain the competing changes or draft a merged version.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Another is writing better commit messages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If your commit message is vague, Copilot can turn a diff or a staged-changes summary into a clearer message that explains intent and outcome.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And confusing Git output is a huge one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Git error messages are often precise but not beginner-friendly. You can paste the terminal output into Copilot and ask, "What does this mean?" That includes things like rebase conflicts, non-fast-forward push errors, detached HEAD warnings, branch protection messages, and bisect output.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also highlights using Copilot to choose the right command in the first place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You can describe your goal in plain language--something like, "I accidentally committed to the wrong branch but haven't pushed yet; what command should I use?"--and Copilot can suggest likely commands, which you should then verify before running.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a section about debugging a failing bisect and getting help with branch protection errors.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Those are great examples of Copilot as an interpreter and workflow coach rather than an autopilot. It can help you reason through what state Git thinks you're in.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And we should distinguish Copilot in Terminal from Copilot Chat.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good point. Copilot Chat is conversational and better for explanations, pasted output, and back-and-forth questions. Copilot in the terminal or Copilot CLI is good for command suggestions. The appendix specifically notes that you can ask Copilot to suggest a Git command for what you want to do.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the safe pattern is: ask for explanation or suggestion, inspect the proposed command, understand it, then run it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Especially with destructive commands like reset hard, clean force, or force push.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can we do a rapid decision guide?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Need one specific commit from another branch? Cherry-pick. Need to tidy your own unpublished history? Interactive rebase. Need to undo local commits at varying depths? Reset. Need to safely undo something already shared? Revert. Need to mark a release point? Tag. Stuck on a commit instead of a branch? You're probably in detached HEAD--switch back or create a branch. Rewrote history on your own branch? Force-with-lease, not plain force. Need to find which commit introduced a bug? Bisect. Want to remove untracked clutter? Clean, but dry-run first. Push to main blocked? You're probably dealing with branch protection; work through a branch and pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if the terminal starts sounding cryptic, use git status, read the repository rules, and ask Copilot to explain the output--not to replace your judgment, but to support it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. One last note: this appendix points back to earlier material on how Git works and to the Pro Git book and GitHub Docs as authoritative sources. That matters because advanced Git is easiest to learn when you keep connecting commands back to Git's model of commits, branches, HEAD, and history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the commands stop feeling random once you understand what they're moving: pointers, snapshots, and recorded history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's the real upgrade from basic Git to confident Git.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for learning with us.

speaker: JAMIE
text: See you next time.
